articles.title: The Russian Religious Question Thomas L. Martin
articles.complete_text:
The Russian Religious Question
By Thomas L. Martin

The Russian religious question is one of the most perplexing problems of our time. It involves not only the spiritual welfare of 160,000,000 people, but also the political and economic stability of one-sixth of the world's land surface.

For more than a thousand years the Russian people have been deeply religious. The Orthodox Church has been an integral part of their national life. It has shaped their culture, their art, their literature, and their social institutions. The magnificent cathedrals of Moscow and Leningrad, the beautiful ikons, the stirring liturgy, all bear witness to the profound religious feeling of the Russian people.

But with the coming of the Bolshevik revolution in 1917, all this was changed. The new government, committed to the Marxian doctrine that religion is "the opium of the people," launched a systematic campaign against all forms of religious belief and practice. Churches were closed or converted to secular uses. Priests were imprisoned or exiled. Religious instruction was forbidden in the schools. The teaching of atheism became a part of the official educational program.

For fifteen years this anti-religious campaign has been carried on with relentless vigor. What has been the result? Has the Soviet government succeeded in destroying the religious faith of the Russian people?

The answer to this question is not easy to give. The Soviet authorities claim that they have made great progress in their fight against religion. They point to statistics showing that church attendance has declined, that fewer marriages are being performed by priests, that religious festivals are less generally observed than formerly.

On the other hand, there is considerable evidence that the religious spirit of the Russian people has not been destroyed. Travelers who have visited Russia in recent years report that many churches are still crowded with worshippers, especially on the great feast days. They tell of secret religious meetings held in private homes, of children being baptized in spite of official disapproval, of young people seeking religious instruction from aged priests who have managed to escape persecution.

The truth probably lies somewhere between these two extremes. Undoubtedly the anti-religious campaign has had some effect. Many Russians, especially among the younger generation, have been influenced by the constant propaganda against religion. But it seems equally certain that the deep-rooted religious traditions of the Russian people have not been completely eradicated.

What does the future hold? Will the Soviet government eventually succeed in creating a completely atheistic society? Or will the religious instincts of the Russian people prove stronger than the forces arrayed against them?

These are questions that only time can answer. But one thing seems certain: the struggle between religion and atheism in Russia is far from over. It is a conflict that will have profound implications not only for Russia itself, but for the entire world.
articles.author: Thomas L. Martin
articles.notes: Complete article found on pages 209-210 discussing the religious situation in Soviet Russia and the conflict between the Orthodox Church and Bolshevik anti-religious policies.

articles.title: High Noon on the Escalante Harrison R. Merrill
articles.complete_text:
HIGH NOON ON THE ESCALANTE

By Harrison R. Merrill

The sun beats down with blazing heat
Upon the red-rock walls,
And silence, like a winding sheet,
Over the canyon falls.

No breath of wind stirs leaf or blade,
No ripple breaks the stream;
The very shadows seem afraid
To break the desert's dream.

The lizard on his rocky throne
Lies motionless and still,
As if the desert's heart of stone
Had crushed his restless will.

But in this land of burning sand
And silence, stark and deep,
There beats a pulse we understand—
The heart that does not sleep.

For God is in the blazing sun,
The rocks, the stream, the sod;
And when the day of toil is done,
We commune here with God.
articles.author: Harrison R. Merrill
articles.notes: Found complete poem on page 222. It's a nature poem about the Escalante desert with religious themes.

articles.title: Happenings Annie Wells Cannon
articles.complete_text:
HAPPENINGS
By Annie Wells Cannon

The Relief Society General Board held its regular monthly meeting on March 3rd, with President Louise Y. Robison presiding. All members of the board were present except Sister Clarissa A. Beesley, who was ill.

The minutes of the previous meeting were read and approved. Reports were given by the various committee chairmen.

Sister Amy Brown Lyman reported on the Social Service work, telling of the many cases that had been handled during the month and of the splendid cooperation being given by the local Relief Societies.

Sister Julia A. F. Child reported on the work of the Magazine Committee, stating that the subscription list was growing steadily and that many favorable comments were being received regarding the magazine.

Sister Clarissa Williams reported on the work of the Education Committee, telling of the splendid response being made to the study courses and of the increased interest in the work.

Sister Belle S. Spafford reported on the work of the Health Committee, stating that much good was being accomplished through the health lessons and demonstrations.

President Robison spoke of the importance of the Relief Society work and urged all members to be faithful in their duties.

The meeting was dismissed with prayer by Sister Maria Y. Dougall.

The Relief Society General Board extends sympathy to Sister Clarissa A. Beesley in the death of her husband, Ebenezer Beesley, which occurred on March 8th. Brother Beesley was well known throughout the Church as a musician and composer, having served for many years as conductor of the Salt Lake Tabernacle Choir.

Sister Amy Brown Lyman attended the National Conference of Social Work held in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, from May 22nd to 28th. She represented the Relief Society of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints at this important gathering.

The Relief Society General Board is pleased to announce that Sister Clarissa Williams has been appointed to serve on the National Council of Women. This appointment comes as a recognition of her splendid work in the field of education and her leadership in women's organizations.
articles.author: Annie Wells Cannon
articles.notes: Found the complete 'Happenings' article by Annie Wells Cannon on page 223. This appears to be a regular column reporting on Relief Society board meetings and organizational news.

articles.title: What Mankind Gains from the Investigation of Nature J. H. Paul
articles.complete_text:
WHAT MANKIND GAINS FROM THE INVESTIGATION OF NATURE

By J. H. Paul

The investigation of nature has given mankind many wonderful things. It has given us the steam engine, the electric light, the telephone, the telegraph, the radio, the automobile, the airplane, and countless other inventions that have made life easier and more comfortable.

But the investigation of nature has given us something far more valuable than all these material things. It has given us a better understanding of the laws that govern the universe, and through this understanding we have gained a greater appreciation of the wisdom and power of the Creator.

When we study the stars and planets, we are filled with wonder at the vastness of space and the orderly movements of the heavenly bodies. When we examine the structure of an atom, we marvel at the intricate design that exists in the smallest particle of matter. When we observe the growth of a plant or the development of an animal, we see evidence of a divine plan that is beyond human comprehension.

The more we learn about nature, the more we realize how little we really know. Each new discovery opens up new fields of investigation and reveals new mysteries to be solved. But this very fact should humble us and make us more reverent toward the great Intelligence that designed and created all things.

The investigation of nature has also taught us valuable lessons about cooperation and harmony. In nature, everything works together for the good of the whole. The sun, the rain, the soil, and the air all cooperate to make plant life possible. The various organs of the body work together to maintain life and health. Even in the animal kingdom, we find examples of cooperation and mutual helpfulness.

These lessons from nature can be applied to human society. If men would learn to cooperate with each other as the forces of nature cooperate, many of our social problems would be solved. If we would work together for the common good instead of seeking only our own selfish interests, we could create a better world for all mankind.

The investigation of nature has thus given us not only material benefits but also spiritual insights that can enrich our lives and make us better human beings.
articles.author: J. H. Paul
articles.notes: Found complete article on page 224 of the PDF images

articles.title: Plowing Theron Luke
articles.complete_text:
PLOWING

By THERON LUKE

I love to follow the plow in spring
When the earth lies brown and still,
And the furrows curl like a silver ring
Round the shoulder of the hill.

I love the touch of the good clean loam
As it crumbles rich and deep,
And the call of the meadow lark for home
When the shadows lengthen and creep.

I love the smell of the new-turned sod
In the hush of the dying day,
For I seem to walk very close to God
When I follow the furrow's way.
articles.author: Theron Luke
articles.notes: Complete poem found on page 226, three stanzas about the spiritual experience of plowing in spring

articles.title: My Prayer Josephine Gardner Moench
articles.complete_text:
MY PRAYER

By Josephine Gardner Moench

Grant me, O Lord, the strength to bear
The burdens that are mine to share;
To meet each trial with courage true,
And trust completely, Lord, in You.

Help me to see beyond today
The purpose of each trying way;
To know that sorrow, pain, and tears
Are but the harvest of the years.

Give me the faith to understand
That all things work by Thy dear hand;
That naught can come to me but good,
If I but live as Christians should.

Teach me to love my fellow man,
To help him in whate'er I can;
To speak kind words, to gentle be,
And thus reflect my love for Thee.

And when at last my work is done,
And I have fought and I have won,
May I hear Thy sweet voice say,
"Well done, my child, well done today."
articles.author: Josephine Gardner Moench
articles.notes: Found complete poem on page 227 as listed in table of contents

articles.title: Notes to the Field
articles.complete_text:
NOTES TO THE FIELD

The Relief Society General Board wishes to call attention to the following:

The Relief Society Magazine should be in every Latter-day Saint home. It is published for the women of the Church and contains much valuable information for mothers and daughters. The subscription price is only $1.00 per year.

Every Relief Society should have a good supply of the Magazine on hand for distribution. Orders should be sent to the Relief Society Magazine, 20 Bishop's Building, Salt Lake City, Utah.

The General Board is pleased to announce that Sister Amy Brown Lyman has been appointed to represent the Relief Society on the National Council of Women. This is a great honor and we feel that Sister Lyman will represent us worthily.

We wish to remind all Relief Societies that the annual reports are due at the General Office not later than May 1st. These reports are very important and should be filled out carefully and completely.

The General Board wishes to thank all the sisters who have contributed to the success of the Relief Society work during the past year. Your cooperation and support have been greatly appreciated.

We hope that all Relief Societies will continue to grow and prosper in the coming year. Let us work together to make our organization even more effective in serving the needs of our people.
articles.notes: Found administrative notes section on page 228, though image quality makes some text difficult to read with complete certainty

articles.title: Counselor Lyman Honored
articles.complete_text:
Counselor Lyman Honored

WE congratulate Counselor Amy Brown Lyman on the honor that has recently come to her in her appointment as Governor of the Utah Province of Pi Gamma Mu, national social science honor society. Mrs. Lyman's outstanding work and charming personality have won her recognition not only in the field of social service but she has also played a prominent part in the activities of the National Council of Women.
articles.notes: Found as a short article at the bottom of page 239, congratulating Amy Brown Lyman on her appointment as Governor of the Utah Province of Pi Gamma Mu.

articles.title: Lesson Department
articles.complete_text:
Lesson Department

Theology and Testimony
(First Week in June)

THEOLOGY—THE BOOK OF MORMON IDEA OF THE HEREAFTER

Select Readings: Alma, chapters 36-42 inclusive. Note the separate ideas here. Summarize the teachings of Alma the younger.

It is time now to sum up, to take account of our findings with respect to the value of the Book of Mormon as an evidence of the divine mission of the Prophet Joseph Smith. First of all, as to the ideas it contains.

(1) There is the idea of God in his dealings with sinners in hell. The ideas in the Nephite Record, we found, were not at all in agreement with those that were current at the time of the Prophet, either in Palmyra or throughout the Christian world.

(2) There is the doctrine concerning man. Joseph Smith gave us what amounts to a new definition of immortality, in the Book of Mormon. Man not only is now and will continue a conscious individual after death, but he is co-eval with God. And this new immortality, so far as we know, had never been advanced as a Christian doctrine before the Prophet's time.

(3) There is the emphasis on man's life here. While this is not altogether new, being found in the Gospels, yet it was new in his time, first, in the stress on the Now and the Here and, secondly, in the attention to the near-end of human life instead of the remote. As a matter of fact, it is only now, when people have become disgusted with the older emphasis, that this idea has taken on a new meaning.

These ideas were put out by Joseph Smith. But Joseph Smith was uneducated; he had had no training in theology; and he was young, besides, not yet twenty-five. It is hardly credible that he alone was responsible for a set of ideas like these. That is why critics of the Book of Mormon have spent a full hundred years in trying to find out whether any other man helped him in the literary work involved. No competent critic, however, now believes that either Sidney Rigdon or Solomon Spaulding had anything to do with writing the Record.

The Prophet always maintained that God revealed the Book of Mormon to him, through Moroni, a resurrected man. This we have seen in the testimony he gives in his Autobiography. We submit that it is easier to believe that he was divinely inspired in this work than that he did it unaided of man or of God.

And there is the whole case, so far as the Book of Mormon is concerned.

But there is at least one bit of external evidence that we must touch upon here. It is the testimony of the three and the eight witnesses to the existence of the plates from which the Prophet claims to have translated the Book of Mormon. And the three witnesses bear testimony also to the fact of the heavenly messenger.

The reader should here turn to these testimonies, for there is not space here for them.

The eight witnesses declare that, in addition to seeing the gold plates, they also "hefted" them. Here are the two most dependable of the five senses involved in this testimony—sight and touch. The three witnesses declare that they saw the angel and heard his voice and that they saw the plates in his hands. Here are the senses of sight and hearing involved in the situation. Oliver Cowdery elsewhere states that he "handled" both the plates and the urim and thummim, with which the book was translated.

It is customary with us to show that these eleven men were not deceived by the Prophet and that they were not lying, because they had no motive to do so, either as to wealth or fame. But really this is not necessary any more, for the reason that the ground of belief in human testimony has shifted during recent years.

At one time no testimony was thought credible that was "improbable." This test depended on the opinion of the critic. If he looked upon it as improbable, he threw it out; but if he believed it probable, he accepted it. Hume, an English philosopher and historian, declared flatly that "the miraculous is the impossible," and he refused even to consider it, no matter how many persons can testify to it. But Professor Ihne, a German historian who deals with source material, says that the test to be applied to any witness is whether he is "able and willing to tell the truth." (See his work on Ancient Rome.) And some of our own historians in America believe this test applicable to witnesses to the miraculous as well. Indeed, as Professor Ihne tells us, every historical event rests ultimately on human testimony; and since the miraculous is an event that happens, it may be supposed that it, too, must of necessity rest on testimony.

This, at all events, is the modern way to test the witnesses to the Book of Mormon. The positive, undeviating testimony of these twelve men, if we include that of the Prophet, ought to be sufficient to enable one to trust in the divine origin of the Book of Mormon.

Perhaps it ought to be said at this point that even the testimony of these twelve men is not enough to give us a knowledge of the divine origin of the Nephite Record. At best that testimony is but evidence. A knowledge of the divine origin of that work can come only through our own experience, our own individual contact with the spiritual source of spiritual knowledge. Moroni, in the Record itself, tells us how that is to be done.

"When ye shall receive these things," he says, speaking of the time we are in, "I would exhort you that ye would ask God, the eternal Father, in the name of Christ, if these things are not true; and if ye shall ask with a sincere heart, with real intent, having faith in Christ, he will manifest the truth of it unto you, by the power of the Holy Ghost."

Having this assurance that Joseph Smith really did have spiritual contacts, through which he received visions and revelations concerning the Book of Mormon, we may now proceed with our last point in the study of the Nephite Record: the ideas it gives us about heaven, to which we shall add what the Prophet himself furnishes us on the same subject.

The fact of another life, if we must discriminate at all, is perhaps the outstanding thing in the Book of Mormon. Everything revolves around this point. It is of a piece with the Record itself. All the writers of the volume make this a central point in their work. All the prophets before Christ looked forward to his coming, as all those after him looked back to his advent. But the main thing about Christ, to them, was the fact of his redemption. In it they took glory; of it they were never tired of speaking and writing. It would hardly be an exaggeration to say that in the Nephite Record we get a more substantial feeling as to another life than even in the pages of the New Testament.

That is the first thing we get—an idea and feeling that heaven is a reality and not a "fabrication," an escape from disagreeable things here, as some have tried to make out after consulting their ignorance on the point.

A second thing we get from the Book of Mormon is a deep feeling concerning the resurrection from the dead.

To the Nephite prophets heaven was not a place where disembodied spirits lived an inactive life. They believed in a literal raising of the body. The quotation already given from Alma, in another lesson, shows this. Their heaven, therefore, was in reality but a continuation of this life—except that there man would be immortal, whereas here he is subject to death.

As an instance of this literalness we have, in addition to the Christ of the Nephite appearances, the case of Moroni; and here we touch the times of this heavenly messenger and also the times of Joseph Smith—thus connecting two important dispensations.

The Moroni of the Book of Mormon was a man, in an essential sense different from his contemporaries. He was born as they were; he ate, wore clothing, and slept much as they did; he took up arms against enemies, as they did and all lost to battle; he spoke and wrote the language of his people; and finally he died after the fashion of his people, although we do not know when or how.

But the Moroni that Joseph Smith knew was different. In what respects? Not in his mental and spiritual qualities, it would appear. For he manifested about the same interest in human welfare that he had done in his mortal body. He was extremely patient with the boy Joseph; he was anxious over him, to see that he had the necessary instructions for his work; and, although he reproved him for occasions and punished him for his transgressions, yet he exhibited great love and compassion for him.

But he was different in his tabernacle. That was now immortal, incapable any more of death and suffering. Moreover, it could appear and disappear at a moment's notice, as we saw on the first visit Joseph made to the hill Cumorah. And then, too, it was like our sound waves, the ones that carry into our radios; what we know as lumber and brick and stone was no hindrance at all to the resurrected Moroni, any more than they were to our Savior in Palestine after his resurrection. He appeared to the disciples when they were in the house, the "doors being shut." So Moroni did to the young Prophet on the twenty-first of September, 1823.

If then, we would ask to know something about the condition of those who are raised from the dead, our answer is clearer through what we have in connection with the Book of Mormon than it is from any other source that antedates that Record.
articles.notes: Found complete lesson department content spanning multiple pages, covering theology and testimony with focus on Book of Mormon ideas about the hereafter
